channel.title: Mohak Chugh — Blog
channel.description: Engineering notes by Mohak Chugh, SDE 2 at Amazon.

item.title: Iceberg v3 Deletion Vectors: Fixing Merge-on-Read With One Bitmap Per File
item.description: Apache Iceberg v2 made row-level deletes possible with position delete files, and large deployments have regretted the details ever since. Format version 3 deprecates them in favor of deletion vectors, Roaring bitmaps stored in Puffin files with a hard invariant of at most one vector per data file. Here is what was broken, how the new binary format works down to the byte level, and why the length field is big-endian on purpose.

item.title: S3 Conditional Writes: The CAS Primitive That Killed the Coordination Sidecar
item.description: For years, every database built on object storage needed a DynamoDB table or a ZooKeeper cluster on the side just to answer "who is the writer?" In late 2024, S3 quietly shipped If-Match and If-None-Match support on PutObject, turning the object store itself into a compare-and-swap register. Here is why that one HTTP header changes how you architect storage systems, and how projects like SlateDB use it for formally verified writer fencing.

item.title: EAGLE-3: Why the Best Draft Models Stopped Predicting Features
item.description: EAGLE-3 gets up to 6.5x decoding speedup by abandoning the feature-prediction objective that defined its predecessors. The interesting part is why feature prediction became the bottleneck, and how a trick called training-time test fixes the train/inference mismatch it leaves behind.

item.title: Native Sparse Attention: Why Trainable Sparsity Beats Post-Hoc Pruning
item.description: DeepSeek's NSA makes attention sparsity a first-class citizen of pretraining instead of an inference-time hack, and pairs it with a kernel design that actually turns theoretical FLOP savings into wall-clock speedups. A close read of the architecture and why most sparse attention schemes before it failed to deliver.

item.title: RaBitQ: 32x Vector Compression With an Error Bound You Can Actually Prove
item.description: Product Quantization has powered billion-scale vector search for 15 years, but it can fail badly on real datasets and offers no theoretical guarantees. RaBitQ (SIGMOD 2024) compresses vectors to one bit per dimension, estimates distances with a popcount, and comes with a provable O(1/sqrt(D)) error bound. Here is how a random rotation makes that possible.

item.title: How I Built This Portfolio (and Its Blogging Engine) in Angular 22
item.description: A technical walkthrough of rebuilding my portfolio from Angular 9 to Angular 22 with spartan-ng, Tailwind v4, SSG prerendering, a WOW animation system, and a zero-backend blogging engine powered by markdown files.

item.title: Deterministic Simulation Testing: Finding Distributed Systems Bugs Before They Exist
item.description: FoundationDB shipped a distributed database with essentially zero customer-reported bugs by running its entire cluster inside a single-threaded simulation driven by one random seed. TigerBeetle and Antithesis have since pushed the idea further. Here is how deterministic simulation testing works, why a seed is worth a thousand log files, and where the technique's real limits are.

item.title: Two Workloads in a Trench Coat: Prefill/Decode Disaggregation in LLM Serving
item.description: Prefill and decode have opposite hardware profiles, and serving them on the same GPUs wastes both. A practical tour of DistServe and Mooncake, the two papers behind the biggest architecture shift in LLM inference.

item.title: SIEVE: The Cache Eviction Algorithm That Beats LRU by Doing Less
item.description: A 2024 NSDI paper showed that a FIFO queue, one bit per object, and a lazy hand pointer can out-perform LRU, ARC, and friends on web workloads, while removing the lock that makes LRU a scalability bottleneck. Here is how SIEVE works and why its simplicity is the whole point.
